concepts.title: Sulphides ion
concepts.conceptBody:
Sulphides $({{\mathbf{S}}^{2-}})$

(i) Dilute $\mathbf{HCl}$ or dilute ${{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{S}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{4}}}$

Sulphide on treatment with dilute $\text{HCl}$ or dilute ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ gives a pungent smelling gas, ${{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}$ (smell of rotten eggs).

${{\text{S}}^{2-}}+2{{\text{H}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}(\uparrow )$

The blackening of filter paper moistened with lead acetate solution may identify the gas.

${{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COO} \right)}_{2}}~\text{Pb}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}\downarrow \xrightarrow{{}}\underset{Shining\text{ }black}{\mathop{\text{PbS}(\downarrow )+}}\,2\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}$

Alternatively, a filter paper moistened with cadmium acetate solution turns yellow.

${{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}\uparrow +\text{C}{{\text{d}}^{2+}}\xleftarrow{{}}\underset{Yelloe\,\,ppt}{\mathop{\text{CdS}\downarrow }}\,+2{{\text{H}}^{+}}$

(ii) Silver nitrate solution

With $\text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ solution, sulphides gives a black precipitate of silver sulphide insoluble in cold but soluble in hot dilute nitric acid.

${{\text{S}}^{2-}}+2\text{A}{{\text{g}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{Black\,ppt}{\mathop{\text{A}{{\text{g}}_{2}}~\text{S}\downarrow }}\,$

(iii) Sodium nitroprusside solution

With sodium nitroprusside solution, ionic sulphide gives a purple colour in alkaline medium.

$\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~\text{S}+\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\left[ \text{Fe}{{(\text{CN})}_{5}}\text{NO} \right]\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{Purple\,colour}{\mathop{\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{4}}\left[ \text{Fe}{{(\text{CN})}_{5}}\text{NOS} \right]}}\,$

No reaction occurs with solutions of hydrogen sulphide or free gas. In however, filter paper moistened with a solution of the reagent is made alkaline with sodium hydroxide or ammonia solution, a purple colouration is produced with free hydrogen sulphide also.

Class A (ii): Gases or acid vapours evolved with conc. sulphuric acid. It includes $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$, $\text{B}{{\text{r}}^{-}},{{\text{I}}^{-}},\text{NO}_{3}^{-}$

3. Chloride $\mathbf{(C}{{\mathbf{l}}^{-}}\mathbf{)}$

Most chloride are soluble in water except $\text{H}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}},\text{CuCl},\text{AgCl},\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{2}},\text{BiOCl}$ and $\text{SbOCl}.\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ is soluble in hot water.

(i) Concentrated ${{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{S}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{4}}}$

With concentrated ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$, chlorides gives vapours of $\text{HCl}$.

$\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{HCl}\uparrow +\text{HSO}_{4}^{-}$

Vapour evolved

(a) turns blue litmus paper red.

(b) gives white fumes of $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Cl}$ when a glass rod moistened with aqueous ammonia solution is brought close to the mouth of test tube.

(ii) Manganese dioxide and concentrated sulphuric acid

When a solid chloride is treated with $\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ and concentrated ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$, yellowish-green coloured gas $\left( \text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \right)$ is evolved.

$\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+2\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{2+}}\underset{\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} ~ \\ \text{Yellowish green } \\ \end{array}}{\mathop{+\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\uparrow +2\text{SO}_{4}^{2-}}}\,+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

(iii) Silver nitrate solution

Chlorides on treatment with $\text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ solution gives a white curdy precipitate of $\text{AgCl}$ insoluble in water and in dilute nitric acid but soluble in dilute ammonia solution due to the formation of soluble complex.

$\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}+\text{A}{{\text{g}}^{+}}\underset{White\text{ }curdy\text{ }ppt}{\mathop{\xrightarrow{{}}\text{AgCl}\downarrow }}\,$

$\text{AgCl}\downarrow +2\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{Soluble}{\mathop{\left[ \text{Ag}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}\text{Cl} \right.}}\,$

(iv) Lead acetate solution

With ${{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}} \right)}_{2}}~\text{Pb}$ solution, chlorides gives a white precipitate of lead chloride.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} 2\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}+\text{P}{{\text{b}}^{2+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{\text{White ppt}}{\mathop{\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\downarrow }}\, \\ \end{array}$

(v) Chromyl chloride test

When a mixture containing chloride ion is heated with ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ and concentrated ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$, deep orange-red fumes of chromyl chloride $\left( \text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \right)$ are formed.

${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}+4\text{NaCl}+6{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{{}}{\mathop{2\text{KHS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+4\text{NaHS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}}}\,\underset{\begin{smallmatrix} \text{ Orange-red } \\ \text{ fumes } \end{smallmatrix}}{\mathop{+2CrO{{l}_{2}}\uparrow }}\,+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

When chromyl chloride vapours are passed into sodium hydroxide solution, a yellow solution of sodium chromate is formed, which when treated with lead acetate gives yellow precipitate of lead chromate.

$\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\uparrow +2\text{NaOH}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{Yellow\text{ }solution}{\mathop{\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{4}}}}\,+2\text{HCl}$

$\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+{{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COO} \right)}_{2}}~\text{Pb}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COONa}\underset{Yellow\text{ }ppt}{\mathop{+\text{PbCr}{{\text{O}}_{4}}}}\,\downarrow $

concepts.title: Bromide ion
concepts.conceptBody:
Bromide $\mathbf{(B}{{\mathbf{r}}^{-}}\mathbf{)}$

(i) Conc. ${{\mathbf{H}}_{2}}\mathbf{S}{{\mathbf{O}}_{4}}$: It gives reddish brown vapours of bromine.

$2\text{KBr}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\to {{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+2\text{HBr}$

$2\text{HBr}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\to 2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+\underset{\left( reddish\text{ }brown \right)}{\mathop{\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(\uparrow )+\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}}}\,\uparrow $

(ii) Manganese dioxide and conc. sulphuric acid: When a mix of solid bromide,$\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ and conc. ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ is heated reddish brown vapours of bromine are evolved.

$2\text{KBr}+\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\to \text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\uparrow +{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+\text{MnS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

(iii) The following tests are performed with the salt solution.

(a) Silver nitrate solution: A pale yellow ppt. of silver bromide is obtained. This ppt is sparingly soluble in dil but readily soluble in conc. ammonia solution and insoluble in dil. $\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$.

$\text{B}{{\text{r}}^{-}}+\text{A}{{\text{g}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{AgBr}$

(b) Lead acetate solution: White crystalline ppt. of lead bromide which is soluble in boiling water.

$2\text{B}{{\text{r}}^{-}}+\text{P}{{\text{b}}^{+2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{PbB}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\downarrow $

(c) Chlorine water: When this solution is added to a solution of bromide and chloroform free bromine is liberated, which colours the organic layer orange-red.

$2\text{KBr}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\text{ (water) }\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{KCl}+\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$

$\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}+\text{ Chloroform }\xrightarrow{{}}\text{ Orange red colour }$

(d) Potassium dichromate & conc.${{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{S}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{4}}}$: When a mixture of solid bromide, ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$, and conc. ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ is heated and passing the evolved vapours into water, a yellowish brown solution is obtained

$2\text{KBr}+{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}+7{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\to 3\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\uparrow +\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\left( \text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}} \right)}_{3}}+4~{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+7{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

5. Iodide $\mathbf{(}{{\mathbf{I}}^{-}}\mathbf{)}$

Iodides are generally soluble in water except $\text{AgI},\text{H}{{\text{g}}_{2}}{{\text{I}}_{2}},\text{HgI}{{\text{I}}_{2}},\text{CuI}$ and $\text{Pb}{{\text{I}}_{2}}$.

(i) Concentrated ${{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{S}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{4}}}$

With concentrated ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$, iodides gives violet vapours of iodine on warming.

$2{{\text{I}}^{-}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{Violet\text{ }vapours}{\mathop{{{\text{I}}_{2}}\uparrow +\text{SO}_{4}^{2-}}}\,+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow $

(ii) Silver nitrate solution

With $\text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ solution, iodides form a yellow curdy precipitate of silver iodide, which is very slightly soluble in concentrated ammonia solution, but is completely insoluble in dilute $\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$.

${{\text{I}}^{-}}+\text{A}{{\text{g}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{Yellow\text{ }curdy\text{ }ppt}{\mathop{\text{AgI}\downarrow }}\,$

(iii) Lead acetate solution

With${{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}} \right)}_{2}}~\text{Pb}$ solution, a yellow precipitate of lead iodide $\left( \text{Pb}{{\text{I}}_{2}} \right)$ is formed, which is soluble in hot water forming a colourless solution and yielding golden-yellow plates on cooling.

$2{{\text{I}}^{-}}+\text{P}{{\text{b}}^{2+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{Yellow\text{ }ppt}{\mathop{\text{Pb}{{\text{I}}_{2}}\downarrow }}\,$

(iv) Potassium dichromate and concentrated sulphuric acid

When iodides are warmed with ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ and concentrated ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$, iodine is liberated.

$6{{\text{I}}^{-}}+\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\text{O}_{7}^{2-}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{Violet\text{ }vapours}{\mathop{3{{\text{I}}_{2}}\uparrow +2\text{C}{{\text{r}}^{3+}}}}\,+7\text{SO}_{4}^{2-}+7{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

(v) Chlorine water

When this reagent is added dropwise to a solution of an iodide, free iodine is liberated, which colours the solution brown and on shaking with $\text{C}{{\text{S}}_{2}},\text{CHC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}$ or $\text{CC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}$, it dissolves in organic layer forming a violet solution, which settles below the aqueous layer.

$2\text{KI}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{I}}_{2}}+2\text{KCl}$

${{\text{I}}_{2}}+\text{ Chloroform }\xrightarrow{{}}\text{ Violet solution }$

(vi) Copper sulphate solution

With $\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ solution, iodide produces a brown precipitate consisting of a mixture of copper (I) iodide and iodine. On addition of hypo $\left( \text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~{{\text{S}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)$ to the solution, brown precipitate changes to white (colourless).

$4{{\text{I}}^{-}}+2\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{Brown\text{ }ppt}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{u}}_{2}}{{\text{I}}_{2}}\downarrow +}}\,{{\text{I}}_{2}}$

\[{{\text{I}}_{2}}+2~{{\text{S}}_{2}}\text{O}_{3}^{2-}\xrightarrow{{}}2{{\text{I}}^{-}}+{{\text{S}}_{4}}\text{O}_{6}^{2-}\]

(vii) Mercury (II) chloride solution

With $\text{HgC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ solution, iodide gives a scarlet precipitate of $\text{Hg}{{\text{I}}_{2}}$.

$2{{\text{I}}^{-}}+\text{HgC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{Scarlet\text{ }ppt}{\mathop{\text{Hg}{{\text{I}}_{2}}\downarrow }}\,+2\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$

The precipitate dissolves in excess of KI, forming tetraiodomercurate(II) complex.

$\text{Hg}{{\text{I}}_{2}}+2{{\text{I}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{Soluble}{\mathop{{{\left[ \text{Hg}{{\text{I}}_{4}} \right]}^{2-}}}}\,$

concepts.title: Nitrate ion
concepts.conceptBody:
Nitrate $\mathbf{(NO}_{3}^{-}\mathbf{)}$

(i) Conc. ${{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{S}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{4}}}$: It gives reddish-brown vapours of nitrogen dioxide

$4\text{NO}_{3}^{-}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}4\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow +2\text{SO}_{4}^{2-}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow $

The following tests are performed with the salt solution

(ii) Brown ring test: When a freshly prepared solution of iron (II) sulphate is added to nitrate solution & conc. ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ is poured slowly down the side of the test tube, a brown ring is obtained.

$2\text{NO}_{3}^{-}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+6\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{2+}}\xrightarrow{{}}6\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{3+}}+2\text{NO}+4\text{SO}_{4}^{-}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{2+}}+\text{NO}\uparrow \to {{[\text{Fe}(\text{NO})]}^{2+}}$

On shaking and warming the mix, the brown colour disappears, nitric oxide is evolved and a yellow solution of Iron(III) ions remains.

Action of heat: The result varies with the metal

(1) Nitrates of sodium and potassium evolve oxygen (test with glowing splint) & leave solid nitrites (brown fumes with dilute acid).

$2\text{NaN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{NaN}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow $

(2) Ammonium nitrate yields dinitrogen oxide & steam.

$\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{N}}_{2}}\text{O}\uparrow +2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

(3) Nitrates of the noble metals leave a residue of the metal and a mix of nitrogen dioxide and oxygen is evolved.

$2\text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{Ag}+2\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow +{{\text{O}}_{2}}(\uparrow )$

(4) Nitrates of other metals, such as those of lead and copper, evolve oxygen and nitrogen dioxide and leave a residue of the oxide.

$2~\text{Pb}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{PbO}+4\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow +{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow $

concepts.title: Sulphate ions
concepts.conceptBody:
Group ‘B’ Radicals

Sulphate $(\mathbf{SO}_{4}^{2-})$

Sulphate of barium, strontium and lead are practically insoluble in water, those of calcium and mercury (II) are slightly soluble and most of the remaining metallic sulphates are soluble.

(i) Barium chloride solution

On adding $\text{BaC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ solution to a sulphate salt solution, a white precipitate of barium sulphate $\left( \text{BaS}{{\text{O}}_{4}} \right)$ insoluble in warm dilute hydrochloric acid and in dilute nitric acid but moderately soluble in boiling concentrated hydrochloric acid is formed.

$\text{SO}_{4}^{2-}+\text{B}{{\text{a}}^{2+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{\text{ White ppt }}{\mathop{\text{BaS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\downarrow }}\,$

(ii) Silver nitrate solution

With silver nitrate solution, $\text{SO}_{4}^{2-}$ gives a white crystalline precipitate of silver sulphate.

$\text{SO}_{4}^{2-}+2\text{A}{{\text{g}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{White\text{ }ppt}{\mathop{\text{A}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}}}\,\downarrow $

(iii) Mercury (II) nitrate solution

With $\text{Hg}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}$ solution, sulphates form a yellow precipitate of basic mercury(II) sulphate.

$\text{SO}_{4}^{2-}+3\text{H}{{\text{g}}^{2+}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{Yellow\text{ }ppt}{\mathop{\text{HgS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\cdot }}\,2\text{HgO}\downarrow +4{{\text{H}}^{+}}$

Physical appearance of Inorganic salts
